data.title: Advantage Energy: Charlie Lake Outperformance Continues
data.description:
Advantage Energy (OTCPK:AAVVF) is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted. There may be better liquidity on the Toronto Exchange than in the United States if your broker has access to any major Canadian exchanges.

The Charlie Lake acquisition continues to exceed expectations. That is definitely one way to make the purchase price look like a better bargain. It is also potentially another way to bring debt down faster than expected.

As shown above, the very valuable light crude oil production is far exceeding the budget, while the actual well results in total are only slightly exceeding budget. But this is going to speed up paybacks and potentially increase expected IRRs if this trend lasts long enough. This is a favorable production mix variance from the plan.

The competitive returns (of various parts of the industry) have been gyrating around with the commodity prices. Much depends upon the planned relationship between oil and gas holdings. But lately gas has not kept pace with oil prices. This had led to a different relationship than assumed, with gas prices much weaker than expected until January arrived.

The advantage of the Charlie Lake leases is oil production because oil has been performing better than natural gas in Canada (so far). The development activity here is meant to give oil production a significant percentage of total production so that low (and volatile) natural gas prices materially damage the company's prospects.

The big deal here is that the returns comparison slide assumes a relationship between oil and natural gas. Companies that balance production between oil and natural gas are always modeling the future to try and figure out the most profitable way to go. In this case, AECO has been negative at times, which has made it easy to drill for more liquids. Sometimes companies like this will then "lock in" a minimum profit by hedging "just in case." Hence the hedging program. At the very least, that hedging program makes justifying the capital expenditures visible and therefore, easier to defend.

With natural gas prices weak, that means oil prices are exceeding the assumed relationship. The next step is to look at the profitability. Charlie Lake wells have long had very low breakeven points, sometimes as low as WTI $30 (which I have reported in past articles among the companies producing from that interval). But as many managements also note, acreage profitability variance is very common. So is location variance within that acreage. That likely means that the Charlie Lake acreage will, for the time being, get a lot of attention. But these companies are very flexible as they keep running that model comparing outcomes under different assumptions all the time.

FFO Analysis

Funds flow from the Charlie Lake acquisition has been exceeding expectations. Now it is going to take a longer history than what has been reported so far to declare something like this a victory. But the acquisition is definitely off to a good start.

Oil and liquids often generate more cash flow per BOE than natural gas. Now this is different from the profitability analysis before because there are some costs (like transportation) that are different for liquids than is the case for natural gas.

This company has played "the natural gas company game" in that it tries to keep as many costs as possible near the dry gas operating costs so that it maximizes the effect of adding some liquids production. The result is shown above. Cash flow is unexpectedly good and was essential last summer when natural gas prices were bumping along the bottom.

Last Article

The last article announced a strategic review process. That may have happened because the market often ignores small companies like this one.

Fixing that can take one of two likely scenarios. Either the company makes some well-chosen "bolt-on" acquisitions like Charlie Lake while using stock to keep the debt ratio within bounds, or the company gets acquired (either at a good price or for stock).

We will have to see what management comes up with.

Tier 1 Inventory

This industry does not run out of Tier 1 inventory over time because technological advances keep promoting inventory to Tier 1. It is correct that specific types of Tier 1 inventory are no longer available in massive numbers like they once were. But we keep finding all kinds of new types of Tier 1 inventory all the time.

This has been going on since before I was born. It will likely be going on long after I am gone. As shown above, there are different ways that this happens. Keep in mind that the typical recovery factor for unconventional is in the single-digit percentage range. But conventional is far above that. So unconventional is a relatively new area with a whole lot of room to grow as technology advances.

There is a lot of fearmongering out there from time to time about us running out of Tier 1 acreage as an industry. I read about it all the time. The fact is that Tier 1 is based upon profitability at the time and place of the usage of "Tier 1." When I was a child, Tier 1 acreage was a vertical well less than 10,000 ft deep (way less, in fact) that flowed roughly 700 BOD and did not suddenly dry up. Everyone knows those days are gone, but the oil and gas industry still has profitable acreage.

Summary

Advantage Energy has slowly transitioned from a dry gas producer to a rich gas producer with dry gas options. The Charlie Lake acreage has so far outperformed expectations. It gave this company a way to get through some very low natural gas prices without having to report losses.

The previous announcement of "strategic alternatives" has sort of thrown a "monkey wrench" into the future value forecast because if the company is sold, many of these companies do not receive much of a premium. On the other hand, if the company acquires other well-chosen parcels or businesses, that could enhance an already bright future.

My suspicion would still be that this is a strong buy idea. The business has made tremendous progress, and there is some upside potential if the carbon capture business ever progresses far enough to go public. But there is a whole lot of competition in this area. Right now, any value is far from assured. It is a development stage part of the business that could be worth something in the future.

Risks

The biggest risk right now is that the Entropy subsidiary proves to be a total failure as a development-stage company. This business is now at a high-risk stage of development. The backers are solid, but that is far from assuring success. Furthermore, any success or value is likely to be years away.

The threats of trade wars and tariffs need to be replaced with something that is constructive and permanent. Right now, there appears to be a good chance of a future recession along with some inflation caused by tariffs. But one thing about economics is that timing was never part of the research because results depend upon what others do.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NRO: Underperforms Peers And Has Delivered Poor NAV Growth
data.description:
Overview

The real estate sector continues to lag behind the rest of the market as interest rates remain elevated. Neuberger Berman Real Estate Securities Income Fund (NYSE:NRO) has struggled to provide any meaningful growth over recent years, and I believe that the fund will continue to trade sideways over the next year. When I previously covered NRO, I issued a hold rating due to the attractive valuation but flawed internal performance. Since my last coverage, NRO has provided some updated reporting, which has prompted me to reassess the fund's performance.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, NRO has continued to erode capital over time. For instance, NRO's share price has declined by about 22% and failed to participate in the market rally over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of about 12.8%. Following the decline, NRO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of approximately 11.6%. However, I believe that distributions are at risk of a cut over the next twelve months.

Since the fund is focused on real estate, NRO has demonstrated a clear sensitivity to the interest rate environment. Despite the Fed recently implementing its first interest rate cut for the year, I do not believe this is enough to drive any substantial improvements in the near term. Therefore, long-term investors may need to remain patient until interest rates are substantially lower in order to see stronger performance from the fund. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that NRO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's fact sheet, NRO has total assets under management of approximately $212.2M that are spread across 34 different holdings. Management prefers to focus on companies with a value-added approach that have strong balance sheets with attractive upside potential. Additionally, NRO aims to look for companies with access to capital and that have staggered debt maturities. The portfolio tries to achieve an optimal portfolio mix with assets that can provide long-term growth and income generation.

Looking at the breakdown by sector, we can see that NRO is most heavily allocated to Health Care companies at 15.2% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Telecommunication REITs and Apartment investments, which account for 15.1% and 10% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up over 40% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these sectors will have the greatest impact on NRO's daily price movement and total return potential.

When assessing the top holdings, we can see many recognizable names. For instance, the largest individual position is American Tower Corporation (AMT) at 9.8% of assets. This is followed by other high-quality names such as Welltower (WELL), Prologis (PLD), Equinix (EQIX), and AvalonBay Communities (AVB), just to name a few. What I like most about NRO's positions is that it maintains exposure to Data Center REITs, which are indirectly aligned to benefit from the growth of the AI market. For instance, McKinsey estimates that the global data center capacity demand will increase by 3.5x by 2030.

Despite the efficient mix of holdings, NRO has failed to deliver a meaningful level of capital appreciation. To demonstrate this, I pulled in the performance of NRO over the last decade. As we can see below, NRO's share price has declined by about 30.4%. I believe that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of price growth. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return jumps up to 86%. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from NRO's ability to continue paying out large distributions over time.

Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, NRO traded at a discount to NAV of around 5.4%. Despite the share price declining over recent months, NRO now trades at a smaller discount to NAV of approximately 3.58%. For reference, NRO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 4.74% over the last five-year period. To better conceptualize the current discount in relation to its historical range, I added the red line on the graph below. As we can see, NRO's current discount isn't that appealing when compared to its historical range over the last decade. As the fund's base assets continue to decline, the discount level has continued to shrink over time. Therefore, it may not be a great time to accumulate shares of NRO at this time.

Since the time of my last coverage, NRO released an updated semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.08 per share, while net realized losses landed at $0.35 per share. Looking at prior years, net investment income levels have always been lackluster, which I believe to be a serious problem for a real estate focused fund. The disappointing level of income generation means that the fund is heavily reliant on net realized gains to fuel operational growth. This reliance has made it difficult for the fund to provide attractive returns, as the real estate sector continues to lag behind the rest of the market.

The problem is that net realized gains can only be captured when NRO is able to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Therefore, NRO offers very little downside protection, and the fund has limited capacity to provide positive returns when the real estate sector is trending sideways. This is exactly why we can see that NRO suffered from a large net realized loss of $1.57 per share during 2022's market decline. This poor performance has stunted the fund's ability to provide measurable NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $3.89 per share
  • 2021: $5.25 per share
  • 2022: $3.40 per share
  • 2023: $2.99 per share
  • 2024: $3.90 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $3.35 per share

Underperforming Against Peers

As part of the fund's operating strategy, NRO utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify earnings when attractive opportunities are available. The use of leverage currently amounts to 25.03% of assets, which I consider an aggressive use of debt during this higher interest rate environment. According to the semi-annual report, NRO entered into a $90M secured margin facility with State Street back in June 2025. Investors must consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated.

The combination between NRO leverage and the underlying holdings being reliant on access to cheap debt has caused the fund to display sensitivity to the interest rate environment. As we can see below, the fund has struggled to get back to its pre-pandemic level since interest rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023. Conversely, NRO's share price was able to rapidly appreciate throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near-zero levels in response to the pandemic. Therefore, higher interest rates will continue to suppress NRO's share price and be the main source of risk for long-term investors.

Although the Fed has recently implemented a small 25-basis-point interest rate cut, I do not believe this is enough to lead to substantial improvements for the fund. I believe that the federal funds rate will need to return to its pre-pandemic range before we see NRO's performance drastically improve. After all, the federal funds rate still remains near its decade highs following the recent cut. The higher interest rate also shrinks the fund's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from its securities. So, as long as interest rates remain elevated, I believe that NRO will continue to struggle.

I also believe that there are more suitable peers that are also focused on the real estate section. For instance, Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) shares a lot of the same positions and approach to its holding selection. However, RQI has been able to provide superior returns compared to NRO over the last decade. As we can see below, RQI has preserved investor capital while also providing a superior total return over the last 10-year period.

RQI offers a much lower dividend yield of only 7.7%, but the more attractive level of capital preservation would be more preferable for long-term investors. Even if we compare a more traditional fund that tracks the real estate sector, such as the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE), this offers a much more compelling value proposition for investors. These peers demonstrate that even though the real estate sector has lagged over the last few years, you can still grow or preserve your capital.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0312 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of approximately $0.3744 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, NRO has struggled to generate a sufficient level of earnings to support these payouts. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($1.51) per share
  • 2021: $1.75 per share
  • 2022: ($1.48) per share
  • 2023: ($0.04) per share
  • 2024: $1.28 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): ($0.27) per share

As we can see, NRO has only been able to generate a sufficient level of earnings in two of the last five years. This is why we can see the dividend payouts have declined over recent years. For this reason, it becomes difficult to recommend a position in NRO for income investors. The main reason that investors maintain exposure to a high-yield fund like NRO is for the income that it can provide. If that income is consistently under the threat of being reduced, it defeats the purpose of maintaining a position.

Since the fund collects its income from a portfolio of REITs, the distributions are typically fueled by net investment income when market conditions are positive. Therefore, NRO is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax burden from the income received. However, the semi-annual report indicates that distributions can be funded by return of capital during periods where NRO doesn't generate a sufficient level of income to support the payouts.

Takeaway

So despite the recent interest rate cut, I maintain my hold rating on NRO at this time. I believe that the fund has some structural flaws that need to be improved. For instance, the level of net investment income has remained very weak for a fund that emphasizes income generation. Additionally, the heavy use of leverage is one of the reasons why NRO's share price has struggled to regain some meaningful growth over recent years. The fund has not been able to provide a favorable amount of NAV growth, and I don't anticipate this improving until interest rates are back to their pre-pandemic levels.

data.title: ULTY And SLTY: The Gambler's Income ETF Has A Twin
data.description:
Introduction

In July, I angered much of the income investing community when I described the YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY) as the "Gambler's Income ETF," but I meant it and still do.

Essentially, the fund has a mandate to seek out high volatility stocks for its portfolio, and then manage options around those stocks. This leads to a list of stocks that looks like a healthy smattering of today's most hyped, overvalued, often unprofitable companies in the portfolio. Current top names include Palantir (PLTR), Robinhood (HOOD), Rocket Lab (RKLB), Upstart (UPST), CoreWeave (CRWV), NuScale Power (SMR), and Hims & Hers (HIMS).

Some good, some bad. I have buy ratings out on a few of these, and I own shares of PLTR, UPST, and SMR, others I wouldn't touch with a 10ft pole (that's ~3m for non-Americans). But ULTY is indifferent to the actual quality of the businesses it owns. It owns them because they are highly volatile, and thus pay high premiums when selling options against them. The actual companies being quality or not is just happenstance; it is a byproduct of the strategy of picking through high volatility stocks with enough liquidity to sell options against.

The strategy does one thing very well, however: produce income.

Its advertised distribution rate is face-melting.

My thoughts on this strategy boil down to it having a negative expected return, similar to many casino games. That doesn't mean you will lose money, for the record. I sometimes invest in things that have a negative expected return if I think I know better (a la PLTR et al.), or if I think it will be fun — e.g., actual Blackjack.

Here's an excerpt from my last article on ULTY that is a little more verbose:

[ULTY] is not an ETF suitable for the investor who needs reliable income, nor is it for the investor who is risk-adverse. This is for those income investors who have some "fun money" to try to ratchet up their overall yield, but who understand that it could come with big losses... there was also a long time when investors didn't make back their losses in income, and the fund was a net negative for those who bought in at inception.

This is best described as the gambler's income ETF because it's for those investors who border on speculators, and like to buy a few lottery tickets. ULTY is definitely one such lottery ticket. It could be horrible, or it could produce more income than it is worth, and make the investor a bit of money.

There's a great argument here that folks would be better served by index-based income ETFs, now that we have such great options like NEOS' QQQI...

Since then, here's the track record: mixed, but positive.

Here it is against my recommendation, the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI), which I last covered back in May. It should be of note that QQQI is still considered aggressive in my book, but far more suitable for the average aggressive income investor than ULTY.

Again, mixed, but positive — outperformance today, but maybe not tomorrow.

A Few Notes on ULTY

Recently, I've noticed a pattern on articles critical of ULTY, particularly of commenters being irate about authors being unknowledgeable about ULTY and its history ("Did you know that ULTY changed its strategy?"). So I want to note here in particular that yes — ULTY changed its strategy over the course of last year and this year, and stabilized its NAV decay problem. The start of 2025 and really March 2025 when weekly distributions began, is seen as the beginning of the new strategy, so if you are evaluating ULTY, make sure to take that timeline into account.

I wrote my last article on ULTY after that change occurred, and did take into account its use of real stock and not just synthetic positions, increased maneuverability in its options positions (collars, spreads, etc.), and flexibility in its pursuit of income when I wrote that article.

My core issue with the fund is its mandate, not its execution. I admire the trading desk at YieldMax ("YM") for their cleverness, quickness, and execution en masse — they run more funds than I can keep track of, I don't know how they do it. My disagreement with ULTY isn't borne from ignorance about the fund, and I just want to make that clear.

I've been covering ULTY for a while, so I don't typically bring up the change in my coverage, but I felt the need to explicitly cover it here since I've been reading a lot of those comments recently.

You can see the strategy change in the chart. It's very obvious in the all-time chart where they made the pivot. The price stops grinding (as hard), and total return recovers quickly.

I do think these changes make ULTY much more attractive as a strategy, but the core niche it fills is high risk, high reward. If markets continue to explode upward and reward high beta holdings, volatility continues to fall (which could happen, smart money is betting against vol currently, just like ULTY shareholders), and the fund managers do a good job managing the options — you never know with key man risk — then it may continue to outperform the Nasdaq covered call funds on a total return basis. While both would be harmed in a market downturn, the Nasdaq fund is likely to draw down less, and provide more stable income than ULTY.

We saw this earlier this year, although note that the strategy change was underway around this time (it was undertaken in phases, with the last one ending in March 2025 with the transitions to weekly distributions), and the market turbulence we saw was unprecedented — most of them are, aren't they?

Here's starting in March, to be fair to ULTY.

Note that even with the above stats the way they are, with ULTY showing lower dividends and a lower price since the change to weekly pay, their total return in that timeframe was still very positive, and even outperformed. That is the power of reinvesting dividends in an unadulterated rally, but I tend to assume that income investors tend to want the income, and don't plan to reinvest it.

ULTY Has a New Sister Fund, SLTY

A new fund from YM caught my eye, the YieldMax Ultra Short Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:SLTY), launched just last month. It is also a weekly pay and seeks out high volatility stocks to cover, like ULTY, only it holds a negative beta instead of a positive one.

In layman's terms, ULTY is short volatility and long stocks, and SLTY is short volatility and short stocks. The short volatility component is how income is generated, as both funds pay weekly. The stock component is what sets them apart, and makes their chart look like a mirror.

ULTY and SLTY carry the same expense ratio, 1.24% p.a., which is high in the ETF space, but can be seen as reasonable given how hands-on the fund's management is with them. That decision will be personal to investors, as some are more fee-sensitive than others. My feeling is that the fee is justified, which is why I retracted my sell rating on ULTY back in January.

This new fund doesn't own the same stocks, but it is short a similar amount of beta (market risk) that ULTY is, resulting in a mirror pattern, but not mirror performance by the number, so if ULTY is up, expect SLTY to be down and vice versa. Keep that in mind.

Top holdings in SLTY (remember that the bias is short) include Nebius (NBIS), MARA Holdings (MARA), Bloom Energy (BE), Circle (CRCL), and Lemonade (LMND). Again, another personal holding of mine, NBIS. Otherwise, these may be good short targets from my point-of-view, but from SLTY's point of view, they are selected because they promise high premiums on their options, and thus high income.

SLTY doesn't make the strategy better, from my view, and isn't a way to fix the issues I have with ULTY, since my issue is the indifference to quality itself. However, SLTY may be a welcome addition to hyper-aggressive income portfolios just like ULTY is; income investors can use these two to express a directional opinion in the market (using high beta stocks as a proxy for the market) while still collecting high income.

It's an interesting proposition, but now we just have two ETFs that are like gambling for income instead of one. It doesn't earn either a buy rating from me, but I can see their use in a portfolio sparingly. A little goes a long way. A 2% position in ULTY or SLTY in an otherwise 8% yielding portfolio can bring the whole portfolio up to 9-10% on its own.

That comes with risks, like underperformance if the income doesn't make up for the inevitable price erosion that comes with ultra aggressive covered call strategies, long and short.

Same timeframe as the last chart, showing the last month that SLTY has been on the market, but with the prices of the two funds as well as the total return. Note that both are negative for this period.

Dividends, Yields, & Taxes

The power of these funds comes from their dividends. ULTY has a much longer history of dividends, but I'm only going to show the history from the weekly change forward, to make it easier to see the recent history. The dividends go from big monthly dividends to small weekly dividends, but that is only an accounting change, not a return change. It actually could provide higher returns to those reinvesting dividends, as they accrue equity on a more frequent basis.

While ULTY's dividend is fairly volatile, it has been less volatile than I expected over the last few months. This is a win for income investors, as predictability in income is an important part of financial planning. The more consistent ULTY can be in its distributions, the better. They will never hit the level of consistency that bond funds have, but that's why we're celebrating the consistency we have seen since June.

SLTY has a much shorter timeline, but here is its month of distributions so far. They are much higher because the price of the fund is higher, for context.

Too little data to conclude much, especially because distributions are likely to continue to change as investor money flows into the fund (it's still very new and low AUM), the managers adjust positions, and market conditions change. In a downturn, where SLTY would be expected to gain in price since it's short, its income may become more volatile.

For now, SLTY's going distribution rate is 69%, and I expect it to continue to climb up toward matching ULTY's near-88% mark as the fund matures. The strategy they employ seems geared toward that result, regardless of the price erosion necessary to achieve it.

While we don't have any tax documents for SLTY yet, I expect it to have the same mechanics as ULTY, since they both employ options-based strategies. That means that distributions from these two funds largely qualify for favorable tax treatment. I covered this in my last article about ULTY, but it's very relevant here as well, and I expect it to apply to SLTY distributions as well:

One important thing to note when looking up ULTY’s yield is that some figures, like the '30-day SEC yield', only reflect specific types of income. Options income produced by ETFs and passed through to investors is largely considered return-of-capital ("RoC"), which does not count for the 30-day SEC yield. That yield only includes net investment income ("NII" or "ordinary income") like the income from most bonds. Since ULTY makes nearly all of its income from options trading, nearly all of its income is expected to be classified as RoC.

...

The significance of RoC is mostly important to investors using ULTY outside a tax-advantaged account.

Distributions that include RoC are counted against the cost-basis of your shares, and only create a taxable event when you sell those shares. This means that the income is tax-deferred until the sale of the shares, or until your cost basis becomes zero.

If you get to zero on your cost basis, new RoC distributions would be considered long-term capital gains, according to this IRS publication:

A non-dividend distribution reduces the basis of your stock. It is not taxed until your basis in the stock is fully recovered. This nontaxable portion is also called a return of capital; it is a return of your investment in the stock of the company. If you buy stock in a corporation in different lots at different times, and you cannot definitely identify the shares subject to the non-dividend distribution, reduce the basis of your earliest purchases first. When the basis of your stock has been reduced to zero, report any additional non-dividend distribution you receive as a capital gain. Whether you report it as a long-term or short-term capital gain depends on how long you have held the stock...

Example 1. You bought stock in 2010 for $100. In 2013, you received a non-dividend distribution of $80. You did not include this amount in your income, but you reduced the basis of your stock to $20. You received a non-dividend distribution of $30 in 2023. The first $20 of this amount reduced your basis to zero. You report the other $10 as a long-term capital gain for 2023. You must report as a long-term capital gain any non-dividend distribution you receive on this stock in later years.

YieldMax has a complicated tax forms list, with funds put into groups for their distributions. Because ULTY distributes every week, it is included in every group. To find its tax forms, you have to look through Groups A-D, as it will be listed in all of them.

SLTY should also be included in all groups, once tax forms are more widely available to investors. The most current weekly distribution from 9/19/25, Group C, shows ULTY at 100% RoC and SLTY at 96% RoC.

Being able to defer your tax liabilities on the income you receive is a great perk of this fund, but is common in most covered call and options-based funds, and is not unique to ULTY and SLTY per se.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I'm glad that ETF breadth continues to expand and investors are given tools like ULTY and SLTY. Even though I still believe that this pair are the gambler's income ETFs of choice, I see their merit as ways to take big swings at income with small amounts of capital, and I understand how they can contribute meaningfully to a portfolio when used prudently — a bit of a oxymoron.

Anyone telling you ULTY and SLTY are all good or all bad are missing the point: they are tools to be deployed intelligently and with abundant caution, but it's okay to play a few hands of blackjack every now and then.

Just don't bet the house. Be reasonable. You might make a few bucks along the way, but prepare yourself for it to be a likely unprofitable venture over long periods of time. This bull run was good to ULTY, and the next bear market will be good to SLTY. If you're smart and lucky enough (it takes both to be good at gambling), you can keep your high income and change your directional bias.

Modern markets are fascinating, and they have given us some fascinating tools, for better or worse.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Teekay Corporation: Uncertainty Still In Control
data.description:
Teekay Corporation (NYSE:TK) is a company that is having a decent year. But Mr. Market appears to be very unsure as to how the future will unfold. Because of this, the stock is unlikely to go anywhere soon regardless of spot rates and other really good signs. Most cyclical industries have an average profitability valuation somehow in the stock price. But this implies a forward look for at least some of the stock price investment strategy. Right now, that forward look is not good.

Since the last article, industry fundamentals remain strong. As the comments section demonstrates, that has a tendency to lull investors to sleep or even to "overstay their welcome." That could be very dangerous to your investment returns. Investment strategies are largely about avoiding potentially large risks that can materially decrease long-term returns. Right now, political uncertainties seem to promise a large, if uncertain, downturn. That makes me avoid volatile areas like this one.

The CEO's comments from the second quarter report show a good short-term, even if weakening, trend:

Spot tanker rates were counter-seasonally strong during the quarter with rates outperforming the last two quarters and at levels well above the historical average for a second quarter. So far in the third quarter, we have seen rates soften slightly in line with normal seasonal trends. Looking ahead, with OPEC+ unwinding production cuts at an accelerated pace, an increase in non-OPEC+ production in the Atlantic Basin, and low global oil inventories, we believe Teekay Tankers is well-positioned to benefit from the potential seasonal tanker demand uplift later in the year.

But some further comments are likely the reason for holding back the stock price:

Looking further ahead, the medium-term demand outlook remains complex due to a combination of economic and geopolitical factors. Uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade tariffs continues to cloud the economic outlook, with most 3 major forecasting agencies having lowered their outlook for global GDP and trade growth since the start of the year. As a result, the IEA has lowered its outlook for global oil demand growth in 2025 to 0.7 mb/d, compared to forecast growth of 1.0 mb/d at the start of the year, with further growth of 0.7 mb/d projected in 2026.

Management goes on in some detail about this "complex" and "unclear" situation. I continue to believe that now is the time to take profits while the taking is good and watch this unfold from the sidelines. My suspicion is that when management is using guarded language like this, there is substantial risk that is not worth the return.

In short, the asymmetric return favors investment losses. Such a situation would indicate that now is the time to raise cash while waiting for an opportunity with far less risk.

Note that this says nothing about how well the company is run or whether or not the stock is now "cheap." Instead, this is Mr. Market looking forward and seeing a lot of threats that are essentially holding back stock price appreciation. More importantly, those threats are beyond the control of management and firmly in the area of politics. When it comes to politics, my crystal ball needs a serious upgrade. Until that happens, I get out the popcorn and soda and watch from the sidelines. For that reason, I would maintain a strong sell.

Without the political uncertainty, things actually look good for the company even though rates have been trending downward, as shown above. But politics has a way of overcoming good industry fundamentals like few influences I can come up with. It always seems to be more important than anything else.

So, with that in mind, all the positives mentioned in the presentation and earnings press release are very likely to be overrun by a recession that might get worse if the political uncertainty does not go away soon. For those that are willing to hold through (which is not me), then to make money, you have to wait until the good times return.

This is where a very famous question about an amount came into being: "Tell me why the stock in question was a buy at $8 but not at $4?" This points out the fact that when the going gets tough, many investors lose their nerve during a downturn. That leads to immediate selling for a loss.

Other strategies involve holding to sell at breakeven or a little better at the beginning of the inevitable rally. That is before it is considered that downturns often have their own personal nightmares for small companies like this one that turn a perfectly good balance sheet into something much less. Evaluating the future of a very volatile and low-visibility industry is not usually the thing that makes big profits for investors. That is especially true when a downturn is coming first.

Now, on the other hand, once the political uncertainty clears up, as it nearly always does (one way or another), it may be possible to assess what comes next and whether or not the asymmetric return favors the investor. But this often requires patience because many investors look at the present and say, "What could go wrong?" Look at those rates! It is almost as if the current downtrend in rates is not sending a signal that lower rates could lie ahead. Hence the management caution at the beginning of the article.

The market often does not completely value what it does not see. The fundamentals look good after all, and those rates are still above historical levels. Politicians could not possibly "run the economy into the ground" by fighting over tariffs and trade wars, right? But this is the first administration I remember that will get credit for influencing its first year (economically speaking).

Much of the time, because the government year begins Oct 1, the new incoming administration has to get appointments approved, and people have to learn their jobs. By then it's June already, and it is time for a budget for the next fiscal year. This administration has blown that "usual" to bits. Results to follow.

Anyone with a better crystal ball can comment below, as I suspect this is where the discussion on this company will lie.

Summary

I maintain my strong sell on the basis that risks management cannot control in the political world are currently muting stock price progress. Any "clearing up" of that uncertainty is unlikely to favor this business at first.

My old "Money and Banking" class is just dominating this article rather than industry fundamentals. I don't like that one bit. I very much wish it were not true. But then again, they "do not pay me" to ignore unfortunate situations like this. I am not here to print what is wanted. Instead, I have to balance pros and cons.

The bad part is the economics going with that political uncertainty are currently very hard to measure and, for many, unknown. Therefore, Mr. Market has a muted response to the "good times." But if this unfolds in the way I expect, Mr. Market will later head for the exits. That is what usually scares investors. Hoping for better is likely not a real profitable investment strategy.

Risks

It is possible that the political uncertainties clear up in a positive way that does not damage the future prospects of the stock. However, I do not like the odds of that.

I especially do not like the odds of it after reading the following comment received by the Dallas Federal Reserve in its latest survey of the oil and gas industry, as summarized in the Oil & Gas Journal article:

This parallels a lot of what I read at the Atlanta Federal Reserve, which updates GDP and actually a fair number of professional comments.

A big risk here is investors listening to words rather than digging up what is actually happening, as shown above. The comment above should be valued from a frustration viewpoint rather than nitpicking the facts. Clearly what is happening in the eyes of whoever made that reply is just plain frustrating and unexpected. That is a big deal and is causing a lot of firms to "hunker down." This is not what is on the television or the internet.

The main point is what is happening for the industry is not good. That means that the tanker market needs to beware. Facts will take still more digging as opposed to hanging on every word of that comment.

I spent a lot of time in private industry resolving frustrations like what is shown above. If it were easy, I would not have had many jobs. It will not be easy this time.

data.title: Astec Industries: Shares Still Offer More Upside Despite Revenue Falling
data.description:
Back in late April of this year, I decided to award Astec Industries (NASDAQ:ASTE) with a rare double upgrade, popping it from a "Sell" rating to a "Buy" rating. I can probably count on one hand the number of times I have done this in my career. In the past, I had been turned off by the company because of a combination of weakening financial performance and how the stock was valued. And during the time that I was bearish, the company did in fact underperform the S&P 500. Since upgrading it, however, we have seen some great results. While the market is up 21.6%, shares of this business, which produces, sells, and services equipment and components used in asphalt and concrete road-building and related construction activity, have shot up 34.3%.

After such a nice move higher over a relatively short period, you might think that I would argue for a rating downgrade. But honestly, even though we have seen some weakness in sales as of late, the overall picture for the company is getting better. And when you consider how cheap the firm is as of this writing, I think that maintaining a "Buy" rating is entirely appropriate.

A solid business at a good price

A lot is going on these days when it comes to Astec Industries. For starters, recent financial performance provided by management has been rather mixed. During the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, for instance, revenue came in at $330.3 million. That represents a decline of 4.4% compared to the $345.5 million the company reported the previous year. This decline in sales was attributable to lower volumes and changes in product mix that negatively affected it. It was only because of higher prices that revenue wasn't even lower on a year-over-year basis.

Crucially, international sales for the company fell the hardest, dropping 6.9% from $73.4 million to $68.3 million because of lower demand for its equipment and a reduction in parts and component sales of about $2 million. In the domestic market, things were only a bit better, with revenue falling 3.7% from $272.1 million to $262 million. As was the case in international markets, lower equipment sales negatively affected the business, as did a reduction in component sales, as well as service and equipment installation activities.

Even though revenue for the company declined, overall profitability for the business improved. It went from a net loss of $14 million in the second quarter of last year to a net gain of $16.7 million this year. To be clear, a large chunk of this stemmed from the $20.2 million impairment charge the company booked in the second quarter of last year that did not repeat this year. But even adjusted net profits improved, growing from $14 million to $20.3 million. Other profitability metrics improved without exception. Take operating cash flow as an example. Year over year, it increased from $10.9 million to $12.9 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $16.2 million to $30.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business increased from $27.6 million to $33.7 million.

For the first half of 2025 in its entirety, revenue for the company did actually inch up from $654.7 million to $659.7 million. And as that happened, profitability for the business improved markedly. This can be seen in the chart above, with net profits, adjusted net profits, and all cash flow metrics that I looked at for the company improving nicely on a year-over-year basis. This brings me to another thing that has been going on for quite some time now that seems to finally be bearing fruit.

Management has been working on a strategic transformation program aimed at implementing a standardized ERP system that should drastically improve its core financial systems that have up to this point been disparate. Even though this may not sound like much of an endeavor, it is typically massive in terms of the investment needed and the time required to complete. This is a multi-year initiative that management intends to conclude in 2028 or 2029 and that will cost between $180 million and $200 million. However, the company has already incurred $143 million of those implementation expenses. The firm has also been working to mitigate the impact of tariffs, including increasing prices on its customers, changing some of its sourcing from other countries, mostly from China to India, reshoring when and where possible, and making adjustments to its manufacturing footprint.

But the biggest change, at least in my opinion, is the company’s purchase of TerraSource Holdings that was completed on July 1 of this year at a price of $245 million. But after factoring in the net present value of anticipated tax benefits, this price drops to $230 million. This was a deal that I talked about in my previous article about the firm. In essence, TerraSource Holdings operates as a producer and distributor of equipment that is very similar to what Astec Industries produces and distributes and that caters to the materials processing equipment and aftermarket parts spaces. In addition to meaningfully growing operations, this purchase opens the door for cost savings. Total annualized EBITDA generated by TerraSource Holdings should be around $29 million. But when we factor in potential synergies of $10 million, we get a reading of $39 million. These figures are based off the $150 million in annual revenue that the enterprise brings in.

Thanks to this purchase, as well as stronger-than-expected financial results, the company has revised higher its expectations for the 2025 fiscal year. They previously expected EBITDA, for instance, to be $110 million at the low end and $125 million at the high end. Their previous low-end estimate was $105 million. But in addition to this, the company also anticipates EBITDA from TerraSource to be between $13 million and $17 million. This half-year of results should push EBITDA for Astec Industries to between $123 million and $142 million.

Of course, it's not good enough to evaluate the company using half a year of results for this latest purchase. So on a pro forma basis, I'm baking in the entire year, which should give us a midpoint EBITDA of around $147 million. That should translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $134.6 million. If we use these estimates, and we also factor in the same pro forma figures for 2024, we would be able to see in the chart above how shares of Astec Industries are valued on a pro forma basis. These multiples, specifically for the 2025 estimates, look very attractive to me. And relative to other similar firms, the picture isn't bad. In the table below, I compared Astec Industries to five other similar firms. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper. And on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, three of the five are cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Astec Industries 7.9 8.8
Douglas Dynamics (PLOW) 16.2 13.9
Mueller Industries (MLR) 9.6 6.8
Wabash National (WNC) 4.5 4.4
The Manitowoc Company (MTW) 25.4 7.6
Wabtec (WAB) 19.5 15.8

Even though I am taking a bullish stance on the firm, one risk that needs to be considered is declining backlog, which could lead to weaker revenue in the future. As you can see in the image below, a plunge in backlog associated with its infrastructure operations has pushed overall backlog down over time. This is something that needs to be monitored as time goes on.

Takeaway

Based on the entirety of the picture that we have right now, I would say that there is more upside potential that exists for shareholders of Astec Industries. This latest acquisition provides a nice surge in expected profitability. And even without factoring in the prospect of $10 million of annual synergies, which will take time to realize if they are realized at all, the company looks attractively priced on an absolute basis and looks decent relative to other similar firms. Given this combination of factors, I believe that maintaining a "Buy" rating is the right choice.

data.title: Intel's Nvidia Backing May Not Be Enough
data.description:
Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) is the sixth-largest semiconductor firm in the world. It used to manufacture PC microprocessors and has pushed deeper into data centers, AI, and chip manufacturing now. Recently, Intel has secured nearly $17 billion in backing, which includes $8.9 billion from the U.S government to support domestic chip production, $5 billion from Nvidia for their AI-focused collaboration, and $2 billion from Softbank.

Through the new collaboration with Nvidia, Intel will create special CPUs and all-in-one chips that incorporate Nvidia’s powerful graphics tech. It means that Intel’s chips will be able to handle both everyday computer tasks and advanced graphics or AI tasks in the same package. By combining the strength of Intel’s CPU and NVIDIA’s GPUs, the two companies can make efficient products for large data centers, businesses, and even personal computers.

While focusing on making the best use of the new investments, Intel is tightening its operations. The company has lowered its full-year expense target to $16.8 billion, spinning off its Altera programmable unit with Silver Lake, through a $200 million cost reduction deal. Although Intel recorded a $2.9 billion loss in the first half of 2025, it still managed to make $12.9 billion in revenue, exceeding analysts’ expectations. Under CEO Lip-Bu Tan, the company plans to double down on U.S. operations and scale back on some overseas projects, and it could be strategically important for the company.

Bearing in mind the recent news regarding INTC, we need to analyze which of these factors will have a long-term and continuous effect on the growth of the company and its share price. The recent funding from the government could mean expansion, but pulling back from overseas projects could backfire.

It is imperative to analyze the fundamentals of the company so that the current news doesn’t manage to entirely sway our opinion about investing in the company.

Snapshot of the Company

Intel has a company rating score of 43 out of 100. It appears to have below-average fundamentals, with several negative figures. It raises concerns about the overall financial health of the company.

Fundamentals

First, let’s look over the price per share history. In the chart below, we can see that the price per share has an inconsistent trend, with the price dropping drastically in 2023 and 2024, followed by a sudden surge in 2025. This shows that the company has volatile cycles, even though the price was consistently increasing until 2022. This might have been due to loss of market share and weak revenue growth, especially in those two years. Possibly because rivals like AMD, Nvidia, and ARM-based players gained ground. Also, demand for PCs lowered as AI and accelerated computing became more central.

Overall, the share price average has grown by about 45.57% over the past 10 years, or a Compound Annual Growth Rate of 4.26%. This is a modest return, showing some growth but far below what I would consider as a high-growth tech stock.

Earnings

If we look closely at Intel’s earnings history, we see a very different story today compared to its earlier years of stability. From 2015 to 2021, the company’s diluted earnings per share showed a gradual overall growth, rising from $2.33 to $4.86. For most of this period, Intel dominated in sales of PC processors and efficiently met data center requirements and global demands for its semiconductor technology. Maintaining its EPS levels between 2017 and 2021 for four consecutive years is proof of Intel’s strength during that time.

However, from 2022 onwards, Intel’s earnings started to weaken significantly. EPS dropped from $1.94 to just $0.4 in two years, and ultimately fell to negative numbers, with a loss of -$4.38 per share in 2024. There were many factors behind this steep decline, including the manufacturing delays, restructuring charges, and heavy competition in AI, which the company was facing. Another possible reason was setbacks and rising costs in its global expansion, which led to financial strain.

Intel’s earnings today and its volatility over the years make it difficult for investors to predict the company’s future performance with confidence. Intense competition in this industry and risks associated with Intel’s current execution plans make the situation even more uncertain. While Intel has proven to deliver strong earnings during favorable cycles, the sharp downfall in 2022 shows that the company requires a cautious outlook.

Return on Equity

The return on equity for INTC has consistently decreased from 2020 to 2024, taking a drastic downfall from 26.36% to a negative territory at-18.31%. This highlights the financial strain caused by recent losses and competitive pressure.

For return on equity (ROE), I hope for a 5-year average of 16% or more. For Intel, it is 8.07%, which is well below the benchmark. This suggests that, on average, the company has struggled to generate strong returns on equity and finds it challenging to maintain earnings. So, INTC doesn’t meet my requirements.

Let's compare the ROE of this company to its industry. The average ROE of semiconductor companies is 19.08%. INTC’s 5-year average of 8.06% is well below the industry average.

Return on Invested Capital

The return on invested capital has also been a consistent decliner from 2020 to 2024. It went from 18.93% to -12.73%, which is a very concerning trend. The 5-year average ROIC stands at 5.79%. For return on invested capital (ROIC), I also look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, INTC does not pass the test. The trend indicates that Intel has not been able to create value from its invested capital.

Gross Margin Percent

Intel’s gross margin percent (GMP) has steadily decreased over the last five years. This downward trend indicates mounting cost pressure and weaker pricing power. The 5-year average GMP of 45.35% is good, though. I typically look for companies with gross margin percent consistently above 30%. So, INTC has proven that it has the ability to reach high margins, but its declining trend is worrisome.

Financial Stability

When we take a look at other fundamentals relating to Intel’s balance sheet, we can see that the debt-to-equity ratio is less than 1. It is marked at 0.52, indicating that the company has nearly double the equity compared to its debt.

INTC’s current ratio of 1.24 is also satisfactory; it reflects that the company can use its current assets to pay its short-term liabilities. Any number over one is considered to be acceptable for current ratio.

To sum up the balance sheet, INTC has enough assets to pay for the debts, if it comes to that. And in the short term, it is generating enough cash from operations to fulfil day-to-day requirements.

Intel currently offers a dividend yield of just 0.61%, which is low. This indicates that investors shouldn’t expect much of an income from dividends, at the moment.

An analysis of the value of the company vs. the share price would help us gain more insight about where the business stands right now.

Value Vs. Price

Intel doesn’t have a reported Price Earnings (P/E) ratio because recent earnings are negative. It is difficult to assess whether INTC is overvalued or undervalued using the P/E ratio.

The estimated value of stock is $8.31, and the current stock price of INTC is $36.02. This indicates that this stock is currently selling way above the estimated value, and is therefore not worth buying at this time.

Keep in mind that Intel is a very difficult company to estimate stock value for at this time, because of the company’s volatility, negative earnings, and lack of P/E ratio.

Summarizing the Fundamentals

Based on the facts, Intel shows a mix of stability and challenges. The company can be considered financially secure if we consider the fact that it has sufficient equity relative to its debt and enough short-term assets to cover its day-to-day obligations. The gross margins are also a positive indicator and show that the company has achieved high margins over the past five years.

However, recent profitability trends show some serious issues. Return on equity and return on invested capital both have fallen sharply and are in negative territory at the moment. It suggests that Intel is struggling to generate a good and consistent value from its operations. Earnings have been volatile and are currently negative. It could be because of challenges from global expansions and setbacks related to those issues, and other factors, including high competition. It is difficult to predict Intel’s future performance with confidence.

We know that Intel has secured substantial investment recently and is planning to collaborate with Nvidia on AI-focused products. But the impact these initiatives will have on the long-term growth of the company remains uncertain. We can say that cutting costs and focusing on U.S. operations are strategic moves that can help stabilize performance, but there is a lot of competitive pressure and big risks associated with the execution of these plans.

Overall, the ups and downs of the company suggest that investors should approach cautiously. And with an absence of reported P/E ratio and negative earnings, most valuation methods can’t be relied upon. That being said, with the information that we do have, it seems that Intel is overpriced.

INTC Vs. The S&P 500

Now, let’s see how INTC compares to the U.S. stock market benchmark S&P 500 over the past 10 years.

From the chart below, we can see that INTC has significantly underperformed the general market. While the S&P 500 continued its steady growth, Intel’s stock began to slow down and decline around 2022. Even with recent investments and partnerships, the stock remains well below its historical peak and is way behind in the broader market’s performance.

Forward-Looking Conclusion

Over the next five years, the analysts who follow this company are expecting it to grow earnings at an average annual rate of 9.77%.

In addition, the average one-year price target for this stock is $24.87, which is about a 30.05% increase in a year.

Does INTC Pass My Checklist?

  1. Company Rating 70+ out of 100? NO (43)
  2. Share Price Compound Annual Growth Rate > 12%? NO (4.26%)
  3. Earnings history mostly increasing? NO
  4. ROE (5-year average 16% or greater)? NO (8.07%)
  5. ROIC (5-year average 16% or greater)? NO (5.79%)
  6. Gross Margin % (5-year average > 30%)? YES (45.35%)
  7. Debt-to-Equity (less than 1)? YES
  8. Current Ratio (greater than 1)? YES
  9. Outperformed the S&P 500 during most of the past 10 years? NO
  10. Do I think this company will continue to successfully sell their same main product/service for the next 10 years? NO

INTC scored 3/10 or 30%. Therefore, investors need to be careful while considering INTC as a potential investment.

Is INTC currently selling at a bargain price?

  1. Price Earnings less than 16? Cannot Be Determined (because of negative earnings)
  2. Estimated Value greater than the Current Stock Price? NO (Value $8.31 < $29.34 Stock Price)

For me, the choice is clear. I will certainly not consider adding INTC to my watchlist of stocks at this time because it fails to meet several criteria on my checklist. While the company has strong backing now and has entered into important partnerships, its fundamentals, such as negative earnings, declining profitability, and underperformance in the market, suggest noteworthy risks.

The dividend yield isn’t promising either, so there is a very low chance of meaningful income from dividends on this stock. The stock also appears to be overvalued compared to its estimated value, so investing now would likely carry a high amount of risk for a relatively low return potential.

data.title: Crinetics: FDA Approval And Expansion Of Palsonify In Focus, Maintaining 'Buy' Rating
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:CRNX) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Crinetics: Earlier Data Release For CAH Treatment Bodes Well For Upcoming Conference Data". With respect to this article, I noted the company's development of its drug atumelnant for the treatment of patients with congenital adrenal hyperplasia [CAH]. Another item I noted was that the company was gearing up to submit its New Drug Application [NDA] of paltusotine for the treatment of patients with Acromegaly in the 2nd half of 2024. Since then, there have been major developments in terms of this program advancement. The first of which is that the FDA accepted the company's submission of its NDA for paltusotine for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly and set a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of September 25, 2025.

The second of which is that just the other day it was revealed that the FDA approved this drug for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. This drug is to be marketed under the name of PALSONIFY. This regulatory approval is highly important because it changes the scope of the treatment paradigm for these patients in two ways. One way is that currently available therapies are injectable, while its drug is given as a once-daily oral option. As a matter of fact, it is the first daily oral therapy option to treat these patients with Acromegaly. Secondly, patients receiving current treatment options had something known as symptom breakthroughs, and PALSONIFY covers this as well. If FDA approval was achieved, why do I believe it is still important to go over this biotech? That's because there are two other shareholder value generating events still in play.

The first of which is that the company had already submitted its Marketing Authorization Application [MAA] of paltusotine for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly to the European Union [EU]. A decision beforehand from the Committee for Medicinal Products and Human Use [CHMP] with respect to this MAA is expected in the 1st half of 2026. The second of which is an expansion opportunity to develop paltusotine for the treatment of another indication, which is patients with carcinoid syndrome. The use of this somatostatin receptor agonist targeting these patients is being explored in the phase 3 CAREFNDR study. All of this value doesn't even consider the phase 3 CAH treatment program of studies using atumelnant targeting patients with CAH. Two phase 3 studies for this program are expected to begin in the 2nd half of 2025.

FDA Approval Of PALSONIFY Bodes Well For Large Underserved Target Market

As I noted above, Crinetics Pharmaceuticals received FDA approval of its somatostatin receptor type 2 [SST2] nonpeptide agonist PALSONIFY for the treatment of patients with Acromegaly. This FDA approval occurred because of positive results from two phase 3 pivotal trials, which were PATHFNDR-1 and PATHFNDR-2. The thing is that the company saw excellent trial results from these two pivotal trials, which were biochemical and symptom controls of disease. For instance, in the phase 3 PATHFNDR-1 study, it was noted that the primary endpoint of proportion of patients who ended up achieving IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0 xULN was achieved in a statistically significant manner with a p-value of p <0.0001 [36-week period]. About 83% of patients who were given PALSONIFY achieved biochemical control of IGF-1 levels at the end of the study. On the flip side, only 4% of placebo patients were able to achieve this measure. In addition, it saw a rapid onset response of this biochemical IGF-1 level as early as 2-4 weeks and maintained it throughout the entire study. This handles what I noted about the biochemical control of this specific late-stage study.

It is also important to immediately point out that the PATHFNDR-1 study was made in which patients switched to PALSONIFY from the standard of care [SOC] treatments they were on, which is typically an injectable somatostatin receptor ligand [SRL] . What about symptom control? Well, in this regard, there was the deployment of the efficacy endpoint measure change from baseline of total Acromegaly Symptoms Diary [ASD] score. This particular score measures a patient's daily life in terms of symptoms that they experience with this disorder. This includes symptom scores for headaches, swelling, joint pain, and many others incorporated into this endpoint measure. The point is that the score ranges from "0" all the way to "70" overall. However, each of these items I just laid out are scored with "1" being best all the way to "10" as worst. Thus, the higher the score, the worse off a patient is in terms of symptom severity. When patients entered the study they had an ASD score ranging from 11 to 13, which indicates that symptoms were present. With that being said, 30 patients given once-daily oral paltusotine achieved a -0.6 point reduction in this ASD measure from baseline, while 28 patients who were given placebo achieved a +4.6 worsening [Crinetics Corporate Presentation August 2025, slide #11]. In terms of Breakthrough Symptom Score, a post-hoc analysis concluded that frequency of breakthrough symptoms was reduced dramatically over a 6 - 9 month treatment period.

As I stated in the beginning above, Crinetics pharmaceuticals' ability to receive FDA approval of PALSONIFY for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly is only the beginning. Why do I state that? That's because the company had already submitted another regulatory application of this drug to target these patients with the European Union [EU]. In essence, a Marketing Authorization Application [MAA] is already currently underway for review by this agency. Even better, this specific mode that was made, brings an important catalyst opportunity. It is expected that the Committee for Medicinal Products and Human Use [CHMP] is expected to give their opinion of this MAA for paltusotine in the the 1st half of 2026.

Acromegaly is characterized as the production of excessive growth hormone [GH] by a patient's pituitary gland. A benign pituitary tumor can be a cause to excrete excess GH. With this happening from the pituitary gland, it leads the liver to develop excess IGF-1 levels. Why are these excess secretions of hormone levels bad? That's because it can cause overgrowth of bones and tissues in the patient's body, which in turn leads to the several symptoms that they end up experiencing. Some symptoms that these patients can experience as a result of this disorder are as follows:

  • Enlarged heart
  • Large bones of several parts of the body
  • Decreased muscle strength
  • Fatigue
  • Blood Pressure increase
  • Thyroid enlargement
  • Many other burdensome symptoms

The global Acromegaly treatment market size is projected to reach $2.37 billion by 2030. This is a very large market opportunity and it remains to be seen if it will ultimately be able to capture a huge chunk of it. I believe it can, and this is based on its mechanism of action [MOA], which allowed it to achieve both biochemical and symptom control in its clinical testing. Basically, PALSONIFY is a differentiated type of somatostatin analog in that it targets the SST2 receptor responsible for excessive secretion of GH. Why target this specific receptor? That's because it is believed to be the most potent target. The binding of the SST2 receptor is important to reduce IGF-1 levels, which is a hormone that promotes growth. Beyond the mode of action, the company was able to make a significant stride over other currently approved standard of care [SOC] drugs like somatostatin receptor ligands [SRLs] octreotide and lanreotide. These depot injections of peptide SRLs fail to control symptom burden. For instance, it is said that roughly 52% of patients say that their Acromegaly symptoms worsen at the end of a cycle of treatment. This, along with the site injection reaction burden, is problematic. An oral pill like PALSONIFY, a first of its kind for these patients, could achieve superior patient compliance for starters. In addition, to potentially performing in a superior fashion when it comes to breakthrough symptom burden they have to deal with.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Crinetics Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents and investment Securities of $1.2 billion as of June 30, 2025. The truth is that this is an ideal company to look into because it has plenty of cash on hand. This is not just based on an estimate I believe to be true, this is what the company notes in its second quarter 2025 financial results. It believes that with the cash on hand, it can fund its operations into 2029. With this projection, it has enough cash on hand to make it through many clinical milestones over the next several years. However, if the company does need to eventually raise cash to keep itself funded, it already has a financial tool in place at its disposal.

This would be with respect to a 2024 ATM Offering with SVB Leerink LLC and Cantor Fitzgerald & Company. This Sales Agreement, established on June 21, 2024 is going to allow Crinetics to sell from time to time as needed up to an aggregate of $350 million worth of shares of its common stock. The good news is that it hasn't yet tapped into this agreement, because as of the date of the 10-Q SEC filing it has sold no shares under it. The point here is that this remains a viable financing option for the company to raise some cash should it need to do so. The company burns roughly $130.1 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $80.3 million in R&D expenses and then $49.8 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks for investors to be aware of before investing in Crinetics Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the FDA approval of PALSONIFY that was just achieved for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. The first risk here is that despite the projected market expected to reach $2.37 billion by 2030 and beyond, there is no assurance that the company is going to be able to do well in terms of revenues for this candidate. Nor that the advantages of being an oral therapy and have greater control of breakthrough symptoms, is going to allow it to overcome competition from current SOC SRLs.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity that this company might have with respect to the development of paltusotine for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. This would entail the MAA of this drug for the treatment of these rare endocrine disorder patients. The CHMP is expected to give its opinion about potential European market approval for this drug in the 1st half of 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this regulatory body is going to give a positive opinion for it for starters. Secondly, there is no guarantee that the European Medicines Agency [EMA] is going to approve this drug for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly thereafter.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the already initiated pivotal phase 3 CAREFNDR study, which is using paltusotine for the treatment of patients with carcinoid syndrome. Even though this oral SST2 Agonist has been shown to perform well in the targeting of adults with Acromegaly, there is no assurance that it will do well in these other patients with carcinoid syndrome. Thus, the risk here is that there is no guarantee that the primary endpoint of this late-stage trial, which is the change from baseline to week 12 in diary reported flushing episodes, is going to be met in a statistically significant manner. Nor that the data to be reported is going to be enough to warrant the filing of regulatory applications globally for potential marketing approval of it.

Conclusion

Crinetics Pharmaceuticals has done well to develop the use of paltusotine, to be marketed as PALSONIFY, for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. The beauty of this is that even though FDA approval was achieved, this doesn't mean that there aren't other milestones for investors to keep an eye on in the next few years. For instance, as I noted above, the CHMP is expected to give its opinion on this drug for the treatment of these patients in the 1st half of 2026. However, this is just going to be an opinion and ultimately the European Commission [EC] is going to decide whether or not it should be approved.

The company is already looking for other ways to develop this oral SST2 Agonist, and this would be in terms of treating patients with carcinoid syndrome in the ongoing phase 3 CAREFNDR study. Everything I have described above is of great value for the company and to its shareholders. However, it has the potential to obtain value beyond the development of PALSONIFY. That is, it has another clinical candidate in its pipeline, which is oral ACTH Antagonist atumelnant.

The goal of this company is to initiate two pivotal phase 3 studies for this particular drug treating patients with CAH. The phase 3 CALM-CAH study is expected to recruit adults patients and then the phase 2/3 BALANCE-CAH trial is going to recruit only pediatric patients. Both of these late-stage trials are expected to kick off in the 2nd half of 2025. With FDA approval of PALSONIFY for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly, plus the ability to potentially expand its use for the targeting of patients with carcinoid syndrome, I believe that it is best to maintain the stock with a "Buy Rating".

data.title: BlackBerry: Slow Turnaround
data.description:
Over the last year, BlackBerry Limited (NYSE:BB) finally turned the corner. The intelligent software company has spent years trying to transition from a seller of smartphones to a focus on mobile and security software. My investment thesis is Neutral on the stock, as the recent gains more than account for any improvements in the business.

Improving Results

Blackberry reported an August quarter where the numbers were only impressive in comparison to past weak results. The company reported revenue growth of only 3% with a small profit as follows:

The QNX business has turned into a solid growth story with OS growth in connected cars and future potential in robots. BlackBerry is currently in 255 million vehicles with a growing backlog reaching $855 million in FY25.

The company forecasts this division to grow ~10% to reach $256-$270 million in the current fiscal year with likely guidance for solid growth in FY27. The royalty growth rate has slowed down with minimal growth during FY25 after exceptional growth in the prior couple of years, going from $460 million in FY22 to $815 million in FY24.

BlackBerry is still half reliant on the Secure Communications business where the FY26 revenue guidance is only at $239-$247 million after dipping YoY in FQ2. The segment saw FQ2 revenues slip by 10% to just $60 million while the ARR increased YoY to $213 million.

BlackBerry achieved these results while cutting costs. The company is now reporting a GAAP profit with operating expenses of $75 million in the quarter, down 5% from the prior year while investing in growing the QNX business.

The intelligent software company reported adjusted EBITDA of $26 million, up to a 20% margin and far above last year due to selling the Cylance division. BlackBerry has positive cash flows now, allowing the company to repurchase shares with the strong $364 million cash balance as of the end of August. In addition, the Cylance division was sold last December for $160 million in cash and 5.5 million common shares of Arctic Wolf.

No Longer A Discount

The stock now trades at 5x forward sales with a lot of the recent rally due to multiple expansion. Worth noting, BlackBerry actually reported sales of $145 million in FQ3'25 prior to dumping Cylance, so the reported number of $129 million was actually below the sales from last year.

BlackBerry is only forecast to grow at a mid-single digit clip in the years ahead. The QNX royalty business has some market opportunity in IoT and robotics areas where sales growth could accelerate; otherwise, the company appears set to plod along with minimal growth.

The company forecasts the SAM nearly doubling to $5.9 billion in CY28 due to the expansion into new areas beyond auto. BlackBerry has very much struggled to drive acceleration growth in these highly competitive areas in the past, so an investor should not assume the intelligent software company grabs a large market share in hot new areas like robotics.

Management guided revenue estimates for the year to $519-$541 million with analysts currently at a FY27 revenue target of $575 million. The stock still trades close to 5x EV/S targets for the next fiscal year, while the forecast is for minimal earnings.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that BlackBerry has made a solid investment over the last year when the stock was bought on dips. The company now needs substantial new growth drivers to play out to warrant further upside after the quick rally to nearly $5. If BlackBerry breaks above the prior highs from the end of June, the stock probably has some quick upside to the yearly high at $6 before taking a major pause.
